title: GEOCHEMICAL PROVENANCE ANALYSIS
what: Steatite Sourcing — XPD d-spacing crystallographic fingerprinting of steatite artifacts from 8 Long Island sites; preliminary grouping of samples to Rhode Island quarry sources contributing to multi-institutional collaborative study; work cited in NYSAA Bull. 138, 2024

Ceramic Provenance — TES XRF elemental characterization of Early, Middle, and Late Long Island ceramic sherds across regional sites to identify clay source variations and trade patterns; multi-institutional collaboration (BNL + Stony Brook University Anthropology)

Rock Varnish Characterization — XPD/SRX/XFM investigation of Fe/Mn stratigraphic layering in rock samples from Eastern Long Island; established preliminary evidence for presence of varnished rocks consistent with Younger Dryas glacial period formation
how: Steatite — XPD (0.1874 Å, NSLS-II/BNL): sherds crushed to fine powder, loaded into Kapton tubing, mounted for analysis; each sample analyzed 60 seconds across 3 tube positions; Dioptas used to generate d-spacing vs intensity profiles; Bragg's Law applied in Excel to calculate d-spacings from 2θ values; major peaks compared against Oaklawn and Ochee Springs quarry reference samples

Ceramics — TES beamline (4100 eV, 6 μm spot, 0.1s/point, NSLS-II/BNL): beam directed toward etched surface areas of sherds taped to sample mounts; element maps generated for Al, Ca, Cl, K, Mg, P, S, U; IDL program used for individual element distribution analysis; whole-map statistical means computed with normalized min/max settings for direct cross-sample comparison

Rock Varnish — XPD (0.193 Å, 64.2 keV, NSLS-II/BNL): samples analyzed 60 seconds across 3 tube positions; Dioptas used to generate d-spacing vs intensity profiles; Bragg's Law applied to identify mineral phases via American Mineralogist Database

Rock Varnish — SRX/XFM: SRX (13.5 keV) elemental maps generated using Larch software mapping Mn, Fe, Ni spatial distribution across varnish cross-sections; XFM (9.65 keV) PyXRF-generated elemental maps showing Mn concentrated at outer varnish edge over Fe substrate
results: Steatite: 8 sites separated into two quarry-matched groups — Oaklawn (d-spacings: 2.43, 2.76, 2.81, 2.92, 3.13, 3.38, 3.81 Å) and Ochee Springs (2.76, 2.99, 3.13, 3.26, 3.37, 3.81 Å); preliminary d-spacing evidence confirmed by subsequent SRX elemental analysis in NYSAA Bull. 138, 2024; results support Rhode Island quarry sourcing for Long Island Native American steatite artifacts spanning Nassau and Suffolk Counties through Shelter Island

Ceramics: elemental differences observed across all 8 elements (Al, Ca, Cl, K, Mg, P, S, U) for Glen Cove, Aquebogue, North Haven, and Orient sherds; compositional differences enable preliminary clay source discrimination across regional sites; Al, Ti, Fe, Mg, K, Ca, Co identified as priority elements for follow-up SRX and XFM analysis to finalize provenance determination

Rock Varnish: all 6 samples matched Fe/Mn-rich mineral phases (Braunite, Laueite, Pseudolaueite) consistent with rock varnish composition; SRX and XFM maps confirmed Mn-enriched outer layers over Fe substrates — characteristic varnish microstratigraphy; Younger Dryas dry tundra conditions on Long Island identified as probable varnish formation mechanism; implications for Clovis period geoarchaeological record
